title: GrowthX x Biologica — Output Quality Regroup
meeting_title: GrowthX x Biologica — Output Quality Regroup

transcript.text: Oh, pretty good.
You know, kind of busy as normal, but that's the life we chose.
What about you, man?
Yeah, things are good.
We've run into just a couple minor-ish production delays.
So initially, we were actually supposed to launch last week, and sort of happy that we didn't.
It would have been, we're still working through the website and all that stuff, but it's getting pretty close.
Yeah, I'm like, I'm like, waiting for the day it launches, so I can just tell all my, like, my wife and everybody else, hey, go over here and order a bunch of  here.
Yeah, I appreciate that.
Yeah, it's starting to, I mean, the photography is coming together.
We just got, like, our retouched lifestyle photography, our product photography is in the process of getting retouched.
It's literally just, like, we're just waiting on house to get on their product.
Yeah, it's different.
That's what we're waiting on right now.
Yeah, yeah.
That's awesome, man.
Yeah.
Well, I appreciate the partnership.
What's really cool is, I don't want talk about content quality and stuff like that, but even to this point, I think you all have pushed us in a really awesome way in a lot of areas.

And so there's a lot of new things we built under the hood that is because of biologica, which is kind of cool, including what we're doing right now that's implemented but not live yet on your instance.

And so anyways, but we'd love to get kind of like feedback and understand like a little bit more like kind of how things have been and like what are some of the areas that, you know, like we can make improvements and continue to kind of like tweak to make sure like we're hitting everything.
you.
Yeah.
Yeah.

So, you know, overall, what I'd say just to kind of like recap the relationship is, you know, we, when we started off, we obviously expected there to be some like pretty significant back and forth as we sort of refined the brand guidelines, the tone of voice, sort of what our expectations were from a content quality perspective.
And we found that, and this maybe was just on our side being a little bit naive going into the process, just how much editing work it was going to require on our side.
But we sort of operated under the guise that there'd be more editing up front.
And then like over time, there would be less editing, right?
Like as the system learned and developed.
So we like understood going in, was going to be like climbing a mountain and then we'd start to like come down the other side.
And what we found was that basically it seemed like we were going in that direction.
And then in August, like things just like kind of just dramatically changed.
It was like, whoa, we're not sure what happened here, but it kind of like went off the rails.

And literally, like, it hasn't felt like, and we went through the latest revisions that we just got, where one of them seems pretty quite good, and then the other one, like, basically alcohol seems quite good, the calcium one, like, still needs work.

Maybe we've now, it's like kind of back on track, but I guess the moral of the story is, and you sort of alluded to this, is like, it feels like we're building the product with you in a way, which is fine.
It's just, we're paying a lot to, like, code build product with you, which feels a little bit off on our side.
And we still don't have, at least, like, haven't seen it yet, when we go to build a product, when we go to build a piece of content, like, it's still not minimal review at this stage.
Like, we're investing a lot of time.
It's like me reviewing it, Liz reviewing it, Liz will review.
For like 30 minutes to an hour sometimes to like make sure that these things are right.
And we're investing so much in giving you all feedback that it's really frustrating when it's like, hey, here's a new batch of articles.
And it's like, what happened to all the feedback we gave you?
And then we know that it goes through some like human review on your side prior to getting to us where we're told like, yeah, there's a lot of pulling of content in that human review process.
So it's like, it's like, why are we still getting articles that feel off?
So like, we want to make the partnership work.
It's just, it's so onerous on our side right now that like, yeah, I think we just need to talk about like, what is the profit expectation on our side?
And like, yeah, just paying 12 grand a month, it just is really expensive for what we feel like we're actually getting back in return currently.
So that's what I want.
I don't know if I've about it right now.
Yeah, that makes sense.

Yeah, and I think, like, I can walk you through a little bit of the process and kind of, like, and when I say what we built, it's not building net new platform, it's custom AI workflows that, you know, you would have to hire an AI engineer and build a lot of infrastructure for several months in order to build this.

And so, like, the way I kind of think about it is, like, it's always good to kind of base it back onto the, like, what is the alternative, if you will, and I think from my perspective, right, like, it's, and we kind of can go through the details, but the nuances here that of a lot of the work we've done that, personally, I don't know if you've seen any individual writer that you could hire for, like, less than $1, $2 a word that would do the level of, like, you know, in-depth.

Um, that, that we've gone through, right, like, and so, like, this is your environment, but, like, even down to that artifacts, to the, um, to, to, like, the ingredients, to, like, like, going, going a lot deeper.
Um, and, so let me show you really quickly some of these, um, artifacts as well that we, we built.

Um, and, so it's, like, down to, like, the different personas, the, you know, the, the proofreading, like, there's, like, so much here that, you know, at face value, I think it might seem like, hey, you're doing a piece of content, and, you know, I don't know how many we've done, like, um, do you know how many we've done so far?

I mean, as far as, like, ones that are production ready at this point, you've done a lot, but we're still reviewing them internally from a medical perspective, but, like, I think we're at, ready to production is around 12, I think.
There's more that are, like, at other.
Stages of development.
I know you've done a lot of work, and it's not to say that work isn't noticed.
It certainly is noticed.
The hard part to reconcile is with all this work, we're still not at a point yet where I think we can all collectively agree that we're getting outputs that are meeting our standard.
And again, maybe our standard is too high of a bar, which is possible.
Liz is a very discerning individual.
She is a former lawyer.
She's a really strong writer.
And so maybe most people reviewing the content just don't put it through that level of scrutiny, which is possible.

But I still don't think it changes the fact that, like, hey, if there's a certain standard that we're expecting, and you're developing all these workflows, and you understand, like, kind of empirically, like...
That standard is, because the feedback's not really changing.
It's like we're pretty consistent with the feedback.
But we're still not getting the result that we're looking for.
sense.
That's kind of the point that I'm trying to make.
That makes a lot of sense.
And I think one of the things that we've done, and I think you mentioned this article, right?
The alcohol one that you said was a lot closer.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
So, and just to maybe walk you through where we are now and why I think I feel really confident that a lot of this will be addressed even more consistently, you know, and that's on us, right?
Like, if you give us feedback once, you shouldn't give the same feedback twice, right?
Like, so that's ultimately on, like, us.
And it's really good feedback for us to take in.
And so a lot of what we build, and again, this is not, like, we build a new product.
This is, like...
The workflow, the logic behind the workflow in order to achieve consistent level of quality outputs, right?
keep in mind, like, the bar here is not just, like, the final words.
It's, like, what is the research behind this?
How much, what are the quality of the sources?
Like, who's fact-checking all of that?
Like, this is, like, hundreds of hours of, at least 100 hours, I believe, of, like, work that goes into this process.
That if you hire any writer or any agency that would go into, obviously, like, even if you do that, but then at the output is still not hitting that bar, that's still not good enough.
But just, like, you know, even if you decided to later on, you know, after engagement is done, say, you know what, let's just try to do this in-house.

Like, this is, I think, good to kind of understand a little bit, which is ultimately, like, this research agent comes up with a research plan and has all this context of stuff that we've done for you.
Right, right, of understanding.
And then it's complicated.
Coming up with, like, a lot of really specific queries and requirements as well, source requirements, and a lot of evaluations in the loop that editor gets back.
It's, like, really detailed research brief, and you can see the quality of, like, the sources that it's citing, right?
Like, why alcohol and menopause can be a dangerous mix kind of thing.
Like, you know, like, these are, like, or CDC guidelines, right?
Then it goes through the drafting.
It goes through adding back some of the sources from the research.
It then goes through, like, a lot of the mentions of the product and to review to make sure, like, it's super, super standard and is abiding by everything.
Then it goes through the product integration, which is a new one.

And then it goes through validating the writing guidelines, which I think this is where, in the past, like, we haven't done as good of a job as, like, is you're giving us feedback, going through and updating the writing guidelines with it.
The right way so that it can be used here.
And then it annotates all the medical claims to make the medical review a lot easier on your end as well.
And there's additional things we'll keep doing, like proofing and copy editing kind of thing, know, like nuances and whatnot.

And so I feel really good that we'll get to a much better consistent place here to where then the human review on our end, we'll do a final check to make sure everything is not drifting and things are good and, you know, and consistent.
And, but I think maybe it would be helpful as well as like we go through with the team, like, and then we're rolling it out this week with all the embedded feedback you're giving us.
And, you know, like, this is probably like that one of the highest, like complexities of content that we have across clients to be.

You know, fully transparent, like, because it's not just like high bar of thought leadership, it's high bar of medical review, high bar of research and quality sources, high bar of details of the product that is a physical product with research behind it that you can't say anything wrong, right?
Like possibly, it's like several layers of complexity, right?
That even if you went out and hire like the best medical writer in the planet, like they wouldn't be able to replicate this either in that way, right?
Not to say that, like, there's not still more work for us to do, but I feel really confident that now, like, we'll get it to a place that will be more, even more scalable, hopefully.
Yeah, and to be fair, we haven't really run into too many issues during the medical review side.
Like, that part's felt pretty good.
A lot of it's really more around the writing style stuff, as well as just, like, overall readability, and I know, like, Matt had walked me through.

Through kind of the new process, which I think you're leading to around, A, when like the AI system is, I think previously you used to like individually write, for lack of a better term, like chunks of content, but didn't actually like ever process and review them sort of, okay, this actually is like one holistic piece of content.
So there's all these issues with the flow stuff.
So yeah, that makes sense.
Hopefully like that stuff starts to improve.
But I guess I really just wanted to more like, it had been a while since we came here.
I just wanted to share with you kind of like some of the pain that we were experiencing.
And again, like we're paying a lot for the service.
We're dogfooding it in some ways, like in building it with you, where historically I've never had an issue with that.
It's just like the pricing is really high.
And so I kind of like flip this back to you and say, hey, we want to like make this work.
We're contracted with you guys through like the end of January.
Like we're partners with y'all on this and we want to get there.
But like, we really need you guys to meet us where we need to be met from a quality perspective.
And like, I personally will give you the benefit of the doubt for like another like couple weeks to like work through this.
But if we're still like not where we need to be, like, we just need to have an honest conversation around, like, are we getting the value?
And when I say value, all these things that you're talking about, they're valuable.
But they don't actually like, we don't need to know like how the sausage is made.
Like, all we care about is the sausage.
Like, that's what we're contracting you for.
And if it's not as tasty as it needs to be, like, that's a problem.
We're fortunate in that, like, our product is just delayed right now.
So like, we didn't launch in the past week.
Like, if we actually had launched last week, like, it would have been pretty problematic.
Like, we just wouldn't have been able to deliver the content that we needed for the site, because we just haven't been able to meet the standard.
Because it's just taking us so long to review stuff.
But, like, we're going to be launching end of November now.
So it's like, we have time.
Let's, like, work through it.
But if we can't in the next couple of weeks get to the quality that we need, like, let's talk again.
What is the biggest bottleneck there?
Because it makes sense.
Everything you're saying makes sense.
Like, assuming, like, the quality is close to what you've already seen this week, right?
Like, with the alcohol piece and a few others, right?
Like, we're pretty confident.
And then, like, is the bottleneck more on your end now?
Or are there other bottlenecks?

Once we get the, like, basically, if Liz can review it and, you know, it takes her five to ten minutes to review an article, like, there is then no real bottleneck on our side, aside from, like, a medical review.
And that's on us.
Like, that's not on, that's not on you all.
It's that the, we just need to solve, like, and again, like, that goes back to the fact checker.
It's like, the fact checker seems to be performing quite well, like, the citations.
well.
seems to
Generally seem to be like quite good.
That is like not where my focus is.
My focus is really squarely on just the overall.
Yeah, like taste, style, formatting, flow, you know, all of that.
Okay, cool.
That's super, super helpful.
I feel really confident we'll get there.
And how are you, I know from the beginning, the engagement was always more focused on the thought leadership.
So that when someone comes to Biologica's website on the first day, they're like, are these people actually legit?
Do they actually know what they're talking about?
of thing, you know, they feel like the authority, medical and health advice authority, if you will.
But how are you thinking post-launch?

Because a lot of our customers, right, like a lot of what we do for them is make sure we're also delivering content that shows up, helps them show up in AI answers, and helps them rank in SEO eventually.
And I know it's like super.
Or like it's a brand new domain is not established, right?
Like, but is that like part of a priority for you post-launch or how are you thinking about that part?
Yeah, it's definitely going to be a priority for us post-launch.
Again, yeah, right now is just first like let's establish a base level of content.
But, you know, I think we have, you know, we're going to have, call it six to eight weeks now before launch that like, I think we can get a lot done together.
And then once we get that content out, yeah, we'll certainly look to leverage you all for that sort of stuff.
Okay, yeah.
The main reason I bring it up is because like it's like, I guess maybe let me ask first.
Okay, so you launch, let's say the launch goes well.
Like how, like what is kind of the plan just to understand from like driving?
I think.
And, you know, it's like just so that we understand so that we also internally can go back and like make sure we're supporting that as much as possible.
Yeah.
To start, you know, we're going to take kind of the typical playbook that any sort of consumer product takes.
So we'll lean, we're going to do a lot of experimentation on meta to first identify just exactly how we want to position the brand as well as the product or the products rather.
We have some hypotheses, but having done this like for many years, whatever we think is likely to be potentially wrong out of the gates.
And so we have, we have like a lot of, I think, space and freedom to do a bunch of like initial testing there.
We'll be launching with a bunch of influencer oriented content and creator content.
So building out an affiliate style program.
Once we sort of get kind of like all those pieces in place and start figuring out what's working, like we're going to start to scale.
Quite quickly, assuming we're able to hit our efficiency targets.
so we'll then move quickly into video, probably a bunch of YouTube testing, potentially streaming video more broadly.
But all that stuff is more like months, three, four, five.
The first three months is just, it's really social.
Cool.

Well, we'll love to, because one of the things we've done for other customers, and especially like ones that were very early sites without a lot of authority, it is like you do, if you want to grow organically, right?
Like you do need to kind of go a little bit, find like, there's kind of like two different paths here, right?

Like there's this like stuff we're doing right now, which feels very like thought leadership, like, you know, really kind of like high end, you know, in some ways, health and medical advice, wellness advice type of thing.
Right.
And it's very integrated within the product.
But then like, there are some things that like, can still be really high quality, which requires like a much higher volume.

And they don't necessarily need to be in the same section of the site even, you know, and so we've done things for like, ramp and others in the past that allows us to just like, publish content that's more like, kind of like, high level informational, you know, like that brings the right audience in.

And like, we're seeing with a lot of customers is like, sometimes like when you, like, even with AEO, and like, sometimes you have like, multiple pieces of content being cited in the same answers, essentially, right?
And it's kind of counterintuitive, because like with SEO, like that didn't used to happen the same way.
And so what that essentially means is like, they might, like, chat to PT and AI mode and others might be pulling from like, dozens and

Dozens of pieces of content and they're being very, very biased towards like, honestly, like sounds bad, but like listicles as well, just because like they're trying to understand things in the industry and whatnot, you know, and I know there's kind of like a fine line with a lot of brands of like, well, we don't want to do this type of content, but, but, you know, all your competitors that you mentioned at the beginning are doing that kind of stuff as well, you know?
And so over time, it's just going to put a lot of pressure on your tack if you're only doing ads and paid initiatives, you know?
so, so like the earlier I think we, we align on that too.
I think that will, will help also justify a lot of the ROI long-term, you know, for you.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We're, we're definitely very open to, to doing any and all of that stuff.
It's still like, I view that as a natural extension of what we're doing today.
Like it, to me, until we get this, like, I'll call it basic framework in place of just like a writing, like getting the output.
That's to map to, like, what we would expect the quality to be.
It doesn't make any sense to do any of that other stuff because it's like we're going to find ourselves constantly going back to the fact that it's like it's still not meeting our writing standard.
And I think it's clearly challenging to get an AI to write at a specific standard, like, consistently.
But I think once we do, we'll be able to apply that to kind of all that other content going forward.
That makes sense.
like, one part that could be really interesting as we start to, because we do need to shape the strategy long term, right?
Like, is, like, something that I've seen others do in other spaces, but in similar spaces as this as well.
It's just, like, almost kind of like a knowledge base, like, that you have a lot of pages that are more, like, questions that people would ask.
And there are much...
Much better versions of those answers.
So the way to think about it is like, what are the questions that people are going into perplexity and chat TPT and asking that are, you know, that eventually will lead to your product, right?
And position them as questions.
So it's kind of like a library of questions, right?
Essentially, you know, and the answers are like highly cited and, you know, and then it's kind of like this repository of like resources, if you will.

it's kind of like if you go to like a support portal and you have a knowledge base of like all the different questions you can ask about a product, you know, like, hey, how do I do this or whatever, you know?
And I think there's a lot of potential there for us to kind of map it based on like volume, based on like, you know, understanding like the different personas and things like that, right?

And like you said, like a natural extension, I think of what we're already doing, where like a lot of what we've done so far, I think can be more like kind of these pillar pieces, you know, but then that can link to a lot more of the like questions.
Which are kind of like some of the queries that we use to do the research that, you know, that feeds into some of these articles already generating for you.
So it's almost like, can we take some of these questions that are part of the research we're doing and have them be kind of standalone answers as well?
You know, that kind of link to go deeper into it.
Yeah, we're very like, I'm very open to that.

And I think a good place to start, I don't know if this is the best place to start from like a technical perspective, but it would be the, I think the easiest, most streamlined is any of the content that has been like medically reviewed that we're good with publishing.
So the team has that, like, why don't we just start with those pieces first as like.
Kind of branch off of that.
Yeah.
And like, yeah, very happy to start there.
I'll defer to you on all on like the order of operations of how you want to like approach that.
But, know, but again, like, let's we should just ask this call, like, yeah, tag me with your assistant, like,
It's fine time in two weeks to regroup and make sure that, like, everything's still kind of on track.
Sounds good.
Yeah.
Well, I appreciate the time and the feedback, Brian.
We're really excited to see this launch, though, you know?
It's like I've been waiting to, like, tell bunch of people about it.
No, I appreciate that.
And be a customer myself, you know, my wife as well.
But cool.
We got a work cut out for us.
I appreciate the patience.
Thanks for the feedback, and we'll get this right and scalable.
I feel really, really confident about that.
But I'll have my assistant reach out so we can set up time in two weeks as well to just do a checkpoint.
And in the meantime, in parallel, we'll start to come up with, like, the kind of longer-term content strategy as well.
Great.
Thanks.
Awesome, man.
Talk soon.
Cheers.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss GrowthX's content quality and partnership with Biologica, addressing challenges and planning future content strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Biologica experiencing significant editing workload; content quality inconsistent despite extensive feedback](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=121.0)
  - [GrowthX implementing new AI workflows to improve output quality and consistency](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=303.0)
  - [Partnership to continue with focus on meeting Biologica's quality standards in coming weeks](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=905.0)
  - [Post-launch content strategy to expand, including SEO-focused and question-based content](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1158.0)

## Topics

### Content Quality Challenges

  - [Biologica expected initial back-and-forth, but editing workload higher than anticipated](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=121.0)
  - [Quality improvements seen, then declined in August](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=175.0)
  - [Recent outputs show improvement (e.g., alcohol article), but inconsistency remains](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=187.0)
  - [Feedback often seems unaddressed in new content batches](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=245.0)
  - [Biologica spending 30-60 minutes reviewing each piece](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=240.0)

### GrowthX's Process Improvements

  - [Implemented custom AI workflows for complex content creation](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=303.0)
  - [New system includes research planning, drafting, product integration, and guideline validation](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=540.0)
  - [Added medical claim annotation for easier review](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=662.0)
  - [Confident in achieving more consistent quality with new processes](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=678.0)

### Partnership Concerns and Next Steps

  - [Biologica feeling the partnership is costly for current output quality](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=276.0)
  - [GrowthX given 2-3 weeks to demonstrate consistent quality improvement](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=846.0)
  - [Follow-up meeting scheduled in two weeks to assess progress](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1534.0)
  - [If quality standards not met, partnership to be reevaluated](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=854.0)

### Post-Launch Content Strategy

  - [Initial focus on social media and influencer marketing for first 3 months](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1091.0)
  - [GrowthX proposed expanding to SEO-focused content and question-based knowledge base](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1370.0)
  - [Strategy to leverage existing medically-reviewed content as starting point for expansion](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1457.0)

## Next Steps

  - [GrowthX to implement new AI workflows and address feedback consistently](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=521.0)
  - [Biologica to monitor content quality improvements over next 2-3 weeks](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=846.0)
  - [Follow-up meeting scheduled in two weeks to assess progress](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1534.0)
  - [GrowthX to develop long-term content strategy proposal, including SEO and question-based content](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1539.0)
  - [Begin planning expansion of content using medically-reviewed pieces as foundation](https://fathom.video/share/k5pjVCmVBctATHszfyrVRQx3g4Pxs1kx?tab=summary&timestamp=1457.0)

action_items.description: Have assistant schedule 2-week follow-up meeting with Brian
Begin developing longer-term content strategy for Biologica
